section_anchor: Wireless as the default, not the premium
brief_summary: A two-column comparison diagram contrasting the pre-2021 market convention (wireless as a premium add-on at +$30–50 with potential spec cuts) against Keychron's K series approach (Bluetooth included at the same price tier, same spec). A coral arrow runs left-to-right labelled 'expectation shift'. The caution color marks the wireless-premium cards on the left. Grounds the argument that Keychron changed buyer expectations, not just price.
data_sources: apps/web/src/content/articles/keychron-quiet-takeover.mdx:Wireless as the default, not the premium — 'the market convention was that wireless was a premium add-on. The wireless version of a keyboard cost more, often had worse stabilizers or a reduced feature set'

apps/web/src/content/articles/keychron-quiet-takeover.mdx:Wireless as the default, not the premium — 'Keychron's K series made wireless the baseline. The K2, K6, K8 — all had Bluetooth as a standard feature at prices that made wireless the obvious first purchase'
